content:
# Psalms — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** Psalms (Hebrew: Tehillim, "Praises") | **Chapters:** 150

## At a Glance
- **Author:** Multiple authors. Superscriptions attribute 73 psalms to David, 12 to Asaph, 11 to the Sons of Korah, 2 to Solomon, 1 to Moses (Psalm 90), 1 to Heman, 1 to Ethan, and 49 are anonymous. Whether le-David ("of David") means "by David," "for David," "dedicated to David," or "in the Davidic style" is grammatically ambiguous in Hebrew.
- **Date:** Individual psalms span centuries — from possibly the pre-monarchic period (Psalm 29, which shows close parallels to Ugaritic poetry) to the post-exilic period (Psalm 137, "By the rivers of Babylon"). The collection was compiled gradually, reaching its final 150-psalm form by the 2nd century BCE.
- **Audience:** The worshipping community of Israel. Many psalms contain liturgical instructions in their superscriptions ("for the choir director," "with stringed instruments").
- **Genre:** Poetry — specifically liturgical poetry. Scholars classify psalms by type: hymns of praise, individual laments, communal laments, thanksgiving psalms, royal psalms, wisdom psalms, pilgrimage psalms (120–134, the "Songs of Ascent"), and imprecatory psalms.
- **Timeline:** The collection spans the entire biblical period. It was Israel's worship book across centuries.
- **In One Sentence:** Psalms is a collection of 150 poems that were sung, chanted, and prayed in Israelite worship — expressing the full range of human experience from ecstatic praise to bitter despair.

## The World Behind the Text
Psalm composition has deep roots in the broader ancient Near East. Psalm 29 ("Ascribe to YHWH, O heavenly beings") shares vocabulary, imagery, and structure with Ugaritic poetry about the storm god Baal from Ras Shamra (c. 1400–1200 BCE). The parallels are specific enough that scholars like H.L. Ginsberg and Frank Moore Cross argued Psalm 29 may be an adapted Canaanite hymn. Similarly, Psalm 104 shares structural and thematic parallels with the Egyptian "Great Hymn to the Aten" (c. 1350 BCE) — both describe the sun deity's role in sustaining all creation in similar sequence.

The Psalms were performed in a temple context involving animal sacrifice, incense, instrumental music (lyres, harps, cymbals, trumpets, flutes — all mentioned in the psalms), processional singing, and responsive chanting. Psalm 150 catalogs the instruments. The superscriptions preserve musical and liturgical terms whose meaning is often uncertain — "according to the Sheminith" (on the eighth?), "according to the Gittith" (a Gath instrument? a wine-press song?), "according to Aijeleth Hashachar" (the doe of the dawn?).

The Psalter is divided into five books: Book I (Psalms 1–41), Book II (42–72), Book III (73–89), Book IV (90–106), Book V (107–150). Each book ends with a doxology. This five-fold division mirrors the Torah's five books — a structural parallel noted in the Midrash.

## Why This Book Was Written
The Psalms provide the community with words for every circumstance. The lament psalms (which outnumber praise psalms in the collection) give voice to suffering, anger, confusion, and abandonment. The praise psalms celebrate creation, deliverance, and the deity's character. The royal psalms (2, 18, 20, 21, 45, 72, 89, 101, 110, 132, 144) address the king and were likely used in royal ceremonies. The wisdom psalms (1, 37, 49, 73, 112, 119, 128) reflect on the moral order of the world.

The collection has a discernible shape. Psalm 1 (a wisdom psalm about Torah meditation) opens the Psalter; Psalm 150 (pure praise) closes it. The early books are lament-heavy; the final book is praise-heavy. This movement from lament to praise may represent the editors' deliberate arrangement.

## How to Read This Book
The Psalms do not need to be read sequentially. They are a collection, not a narrative. Useful approaches:

**By type:** Group similar psalms — laments (3, 13, 22, 42–43, 88), praise (8, 19, 96, 100, 145–150), thanksgiving (30, 34, 116), royal (2, 72, 110), pilgrimage (120–134). **By structure:** Hebrew poetry operates by parallelism — the second line relates to the first through synonymity ("The heavens declare / the skies proclaim"), contrast ("The wicked borrow and do not repay / but the righteous give generously"), or extension (the second line advances the thought). Recognizing parallelism opens the poetry.

**Selah** appears 71 times. Its meaning is unknown. Proposed translations include "pause," "musical interlude," "lift up" (voices or instruments), and "forever." The Septuagint translates it as diapsalma ("instrumental interlude"). The uncertainty is genuine.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
The Psalms are the most quoted Old Testament book in the New Testament — with over 100 direct citations and many more allusions. Psalm 22:1 ("My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?") appears on Jesus' lips at the cross (Matthew 27:46, Mark 15:34). Psalm 110:1 ("YHWH said to my lord, 'Sit at my right hand'") is the single most cited Old Testament verse in the NT. Psalm 118:22 ("the stone the builders rejected") is applied to Jesus in all four Gospels, Acts, and 1 Peter.

The Great Psalms Scroll (11QPs, c. 50 CE) from Qumran Cave 11 contains 41 canonical psalms plus additional compositions (including Psalm 151, Psalms 154–155, and a prose note about David's compositions). The order differs from the Masoretic Psalter, particularly in Books IV and V. This demonstrates that the collection's arrangement was still fluid in the 1st century BCE/CE.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **Psalm 82 describes a divine council** where the deity judges other gods: "God stands in the divine assembly; among the gods he renders judgment" (82:1). This reflects a polytheistic background (or at least divine council mythology) that the psalm then subverts: "you are gods... but you will die like mortals" (82:6–7). Jesus quotes verse 6 in John 10:34.
- **The acrostic psalms (9–10, 25, 34, 37, 111, 112, 119, 145)** are organized by the Hebrew alphabet. Psalm 119 is the most elaborate: 176 verses, 22 stanzas of 8 verses each, with every verse in a stanza beginning with the same Hebrew letter. This structure is invisible in translation.
- **Psalm 88 ends in darkness** — "darkness is my closest friend" (88:18). It is the only psalm with no turn toward hope. The collection includes a prayer with no resolution.
- **The imprecatory psalms (35, 58, 69, 109, 137)** contain violent wishes — "happy is the one who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks" (137:9). These are in the text. Different reading traditions handle them differently.
- **"The LORD is my shepherd" (Psalm 23)** uses pastoral imagery from the semi-arid landscape of Judah. The "green pastures" (ne'ot deshe) are seasonal, not permanent. The "valley of the shadow of death" (tsalmawet) may be a compound word meaning "deep darkness" rather than specifically "death-shadow." The philology is debated.
- **Psalm 151 exists in the Septuagint and in the Great Psalms Scroll from Qumran** but not in the Masoretic Psalter. It describes David's selection as king over his brothers. It is canonical in Eastern Orthodox traditions.
- **The Hallel psalms (113–118)** were sung at Passover. The Mishnah (Pesachim 10:5–7) describes their liturgical use, and Matthew 26:30 / Mark 14:26 note that Jesus and his disciples "sang a hymn" after the Last Supper — almost certainly from this collection.
- **The Dead Sea Scrolls contain more manuscripts of Psalms (over 35) than any other biblical book**, indicating its centrality in Second Temple worship and study.

## Timeline
- **c. 1400–1200 BCE:** Ugaritic poetry from Ras Shamra — parallels to Psalm 29
- **c. 1350 BCE:** Great Hymn to the Aten — parallels to Psalm 104
- **c. 1000 BCE:** Earliest psalms, possibly from the Davidic era
- **c. 587–538 BCE:** Exile psalms (e.g., Psalm 137)
- **6th–3rd century BCE:** Post-exilic psalms and editorial arrangement
- **c. 50 CE:** Great Psalms Scroll (11QPs) — variant ordering, additional compositions
- **Over 35 Psalms manuscripts at Qumran** — the most attested biblical book
- **4th century CE:** Codex Vaticanus, Codex Sinaiticus (Greek text includes Psalm 151)
- **c. 1008 CE:** Leningrad Codex (150 psalms)

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
